topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt alien in my hands, the ink faded to a ghostly smudge. It was tucked inside a forgotten book, a relic from a time I barely registered. My fingers traced the looping, formal script, and a cold knot tightened in my chest. It was my father’s handwriting, the same precise slant he used on everything important. The letter detailed his departure from the firm, dated forty years ago. Reading the words—the polite, almost detached language of professional severance—sent a tremor through me. I dropped it onto the dusty floorboards, the sound surprisingly loud in the silence of the attic. I stood there, breathing shallowly, my gaze fixed on the paper as if it held some active poison. Every muscle in my jaw felt clenched, and I couldn't seem to draw a full breath. The air in the room seemed thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

I found the envelope tucked beneath a stack of old tax documents. The seal was broken, the paper thin as tissue. When I unfolded the contents, the familiar, elegant scrawl of my mother’s hand appeared, followed by my father’s signature. It was a resignation notice. The sheer weight of that document pressed down on my shoulders, making it hard to stand straight. I reread the dates, the reasons cited, the formal farewell. A sharp, involuntary shudder ran through my frame. My hands began to shake, the fine tremor spreading up my arms. I paced the small room, back and forth, the floorboards protesting beneath my weight. Each step felt heavy, weighted by an unspoken history I wasn't equipped to handle. I stopped by the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights, feeling a profound internal dissonance, a churning emptiness in my gut.

[story 3]

It was a mundane discovery, an accidental find amidst the clutter of inherited belongings. A folded piece of stationery, smelling faintly of aged paper and pipe tobacco, lay waiting. When I finally opened it, the typed words—my father’s resignation—slammed into my awareness with unexpected force. The silence of the afternoon amplified the sound of my own ragged breathing. I held the letter close to my face, the texture rough against my skin. A strange heat rose in my cheeks, quickly followed by a chilling numbness. I sank onto the edge of the bed, my limbs feeling heavy and unresponsive. I kept glancing up, expecting someone to materialize, expecting the past to rush back in with physical force. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges, and I squeezed my eyes shut, fighting the overwhelming urge to simply drop the evidence and run.
